title: Global User-Level CLAUDE.md
description: A personal ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md that applies across all projects. Sets your coding philosophy, preferred tools, and cross-project conventions once.
prompt:
# Global Development Preferences

## Philosophy
- Prefer simple, readable solutions over clever ones
- Fail fast — surface errors early and loudly
- Read and understand existing code before modifying it
- If you need to explain why it works, it's too complex

## Tools & Package Managers
- Node.js: use `pnpm` (never npm or yarn)
- Python: use `uv` for environment and package management
- Rust: use `cargo` with `clippy` enabled
- Formatting: run prettier/black/rustfmt before finishing

## Code Style
- No commented-out code in commits
- No `console.log` / `print` debug statements left in
- Descriptive variable names — no single letters except loop counters
- Functions do one thing

## Prohibited Behaviors
- Never bypass pre-commit hooks (`--no-verify`)
- Never commit non-compiling code
- Never silently swallow exceptions
- Never hardcode credentials or secrets

## Pre-Task Checklist
- Read relevant existing code before writing new code
- Check if a utility already exists before creating a new one
- Understand the naming conventions in use

## Post-Task Checklist
- Run format, lint, and type-check
- Verify tests pass
- Remove any temporary debug output
- Update documentation if behavior changed

## Communication Style
- Skip pleasantries — deliver solutions directly
- If blocked after 3 attempts, stop and explain the blocker
- Prefer a working solution over a perfect explanation

title: React / Next.js Frontend CLAUDE.md
description: For React and Next.js projects with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and modern tooling. Covers component patterns, state management, and testing.
prompt:
# React / Next.js Project

## Tech Stack
- React 19, Next.js 15 (App Router)
- TypeScript (strict mode)
- Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui
- TanStack Query for server state
- Zustand for client state
- Vitest + React Testing Library

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev          # Start dev server
pnpm build        # Production build
pnpm test         # Run tests
pnpm lint         # ESLint
pnpm type-check   # tsc --noEmit
```

## Project Structure
```
src/
  app/          # Next.js App Router pages
  components/   # Shared UI components
  hooks/        # Custom React hooks
  lib/          # Utilities and helpers
  store/        # Zustand stores
  types/        # TypeScript interfaces
```

## Component Rules
- Functional components only — no class components
- Co-locate component styles, tests, and hooks
- Export named exports, not default exports (except page.tsx)
- Props interfaces named `[Component]Props`

## State Management
- Server state: TanStack Query (`useQuery`, `useMutation`)
- Global client state: Zustand
- Local UI state: `useState`/`useReducer`
- Never mix server and client state patterns

## Styling
- Tailwind utility classes only — no inline styles
- Use `cn()` from `lib/utils` for conditional classes
- Follow shadcn/ui component patterns for new components

## Forbidden Patterns
- No `any` type without a comment explaining why
- No direct DOM manipulation
- No `useEffect` for data fetching (use TanStack Query)
- No prop drilling beyond 2 levels (use context or store)

title: TypeScript / Node.js Backend CLAUDE.md
description: For TypeScript-based Node.js APIs, backend services, and libraries. Enforces strict typing, ESM modules, and clean error handling.
prompt:
# TypeScript / Node.js Backend

## Tech Stack
- Node.js 22 LTS, TypeScript 5.x (strict)
- Fastify for HTTP server
- Drizzle ORM + PostgreSQL
- Vitest for testing
- Biome for linting and formatting

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev          # ts-node-dev watch
pnpm build        # tsc
pnpm start        # node dist/index.js
pnpm test         # vitest run
pnpm test:watch   # vitest
```

## Project Structure
```
src/
  routes/       # Route handlers
  services/     # Business logic
  repositories/ # Data access layer
  middleware/   # Fastify middleware
  types/        # Shared TypeScript types
  utils/        # Pure utility functions
dist/           # Compiled output
```

## Type Safety Rules
- No `any` — use `unknown` and narrow with type guards
- No `@ts-ignore` — fix the type error
- All exported functions must have explicit return types
- Use Zod for runtime validation at API boundaries

## Error Handling
- Use Result types for expected failures
- Throw only for unexpected/programmer errors
- Never catch and swallow errors silently
- Always include context when re-throwing

## Module System
- ESM only (`"type": "module"` in package.json)
- Path aliases configured in tsconfig.json (`@/` → `src/`)
- Never use CommonJS `require()`

## Forbidden Patterns
- No `var` — use `const` or `let`
- No callbacks — use async/await
- No process.exit() in library code
- No hardcoded ports/URLs — use environment variables

title: Python / FastAPI Backend CLAUDE.md
description: For Python web APIs and backend services using FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, and the modern Python ecosystem with uv and ruff.
prompt:
# Python / FastAPI Backend

## Tech Stack
- Python 3.12+
- FastAPI + Pydantic v2
- SQLAlchemy 2.0 (async) + Alembic
- uv for package management
- ruff for linting and formatting
- pytest for testing

## Commands
```bash
uv sync                          # Install dependencies
uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload  # Dev server
uv run pytest                    # Run tests
uv run pytest --cov=app          # With coverage
uv run alembic upgrade head      # Apply migrations
uv run alembic revision --autogenerate -m "description"  # New migration
```

## Project Structure
```
app/
  api/          # Route handlers (routers)
  core/         # Config, security, database
  models/       # SQLAlchemy models
  schemas/      # Pydantic schemas
  services/     # Business logic
  repositories/ # Data access
tests/
alembic/
```

## Type Annotations
- Full type annotations required on all functions
- Use Pydantic models for all request/response schemas
- Never use `Dict`, `List` — use `dict`, `list` (Python 3.10+ style)

## Error Handling
- Use specific exception types — never bare `except:`
- Raise `HTTPException` for API-level errors
- Custom exception classes inherit from base `AppException`

## Async Rules
- All route handlers must be `async def`
- Use `asyncpg`-backed SQLAlchemy for DB operations
- Never mix sync and async DB calls

## Forbidden Patterns
- No mutable default arguments
- No wildcard imports (`from module import *`)
- No print() in production code — use `logging`
- No hardcoded secrets — use `pydantic-settings`

title: Rust Systems Programming CLAUDE.md
description: For Rust systems software, CLI tools, and performance-critical services. Enforces idiomatic error handling, safety rules, and documentation standards.
prompt:
# Rust Systems Project

## Tech Stack
- Rust (stable, MSRV: 1.80)
- tokio for async I/O
- serde + serde_json for serialization
- thiserror for custom errors, anyhow for application errors
- clap for CLI argument parsing
- tracing for structured logging

## Commands
```bash
cargo build           # Debug build
cargo build --release # Release build
cargo test            # Run tests
cargo clippy          # Linting
cargo fmt             # Formatting
cargo deny check      # Dependency audit
```

## Code Style
- snake_case for functions and variables
- PascalCase for types, traits, and enums
- SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants
- 4-space indentation, 100-char line limit

## Error Handling
- No `.unwrap()` in production code — use `?` or explicit handling
- Library code: use `thiserror` with descriptive error variants
- Application code: use `anyhow` for context-rich errors
- Always add context with `.context("what was happening")`

## Safety Rules
- No `unsafe` without a `// SAFETY:` comment explaining invariants
- No `unsafe` without review — justify in PR description
- Prefer safe abstractions over raw pointers

## Async (tokio)
- Use `tokio::spawn` for independent tasks
- Always handle `JoinError` from spawned tasks
- Pass `CancellationToken` for graceful shutdown

## Documentation
- All `pub` items need doc comments (`///`)
- Include `# Examples` section with runnable examples
- Document panics with `# Panics` and errors with `# Errors`

## Forbidden Patterns
- No commented-out code
- No `println!` debug output in library crates
- No ignoring compiler warnings in production

title: Go / Golang Backend CLAUDE.md
description: For Go microservices, CLIs, and backend systems. Enforces stdlib-first philosophy, table-driven tests, and idiomatic error wrapping.
prompt:
# Go Backend Project

## Tech Stack
- Go 1.23+
- stdlib-first — introduce external deps only with clear justification
- slog for structured logging
- pgx for PostgreSQL
- golangci-lint for linting

## Commands
```bash
go build ./...        # Build all packages
go test ./...         # Run all tests
go test -race ./...   # Race condition detection
golangci-lint run     # Lint
gofmt -w .            # Format
go vet ./...          # Vet
govulncheck ./...     # Vulnerability check
```

## Project Structure
```
cmd/
  server/       # Main entry point
internal/
  handler/      # HTTP handlers
  service/      # Business logic
  repository/   # Data access
  model/        # Domain models
pkg/            # Exported packages (if any)
```

## Code Style
- No name stuttering (`user.UserID` → `user.ID`)
- Composition over inheritance
- Accept interfaces, return concrete types
- Keep functions small and focused

## Error Handling
- Wrap errors with `fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)`
- Use `errors.Is` / `errors.As` for control flow
- Define sentinel errors per package with `var ErrNotFound = errors.New(...)`
- Never discard errors silently

## Context Rules
- `context.Context` is always the first parameter
- Never store context in a struct field
- Always respect context cancellation

## Testing
- Table-driven tests mandatory for any logic with multiple cases
- Always use `-race` flag in CI
- Mark parallel-safe tests with `t.Parallel()`
- Use `testcontainers-go` for integration tests with real databases

## Concurrency
- Senders close channels, never receivers
- Use `errgroup` for fan-out/fan-in patterns
- Always tie goroutines to a `context.Context` for lifecycle management

## Forbidden Patterns
- No `panic()` in library code
- No global mutable state
- No magic numbers — use named constants

title: Java / Spring Boot CLAUDE.md
description: For Java enterprise applications with Spring Boot, Maven/Gradle, JPA, and the JVM ecosystem.
prompt:
# Java / Spring Boot Project

## Tech Stack
- Java 21 (LTS) with virtual threads
- Spring Boot 3.x
- Spring Data JPA + Hibernate
- Flyway for migrations
- JUnit 5 + Mockito + AssertJ
- Maven (use `./mvnw` wrapper)

## Commands
```bash
./mvnw clean install         # Build and test
./mvnw spring-boot:run       # Dev server
./mvnw test                  # Tests only
./mvnw test -pl module-name  # Specific module
./mvnw verify -P integration # Integration tests
```

## Project Structure
```
src/main/java/com/company/app/
  controller/    # REST controllers (@RestController)
  service/       # Business logic (@Service)
  repository/    # Data access (@Repository)
  model/entity/  # JPA entities
  model/dto/     # Request/Response DTOs
  config/        # Configuration classes
src/main/resources/
  db/migration/  # Flyway SQL migrations
```

## Architecture Rules
- Layered architecture: Controller → Service → Repository
- DTOs at API boundary — never expose JPA entities directly
- Services are transactional (`@Transactional`)
- Repositories extend `JpaRepository`

## Testing
- Unit tests: mock dependencies with Mockito
- Integration tests: use `@SpringBootTest` + TestContainers
- Naming: `MethodName_StateUnderTest_ExpectedBehavior`
- Coverage threshold: 80% line coverage

## Code Style
- Google Java Style Guide
- Checkstyle enforced in CI
- Records for immutable DTOs (Java 16+)
- Use `Optional` for nullable returns — never return `null`

## Forbidden Patterns
- No field injection (`@Autowired` on fields) — use constructor injection
- No business logic in controllers
- No direct SQL in services — use repositories
- No `System.out.println` — use SLF4J logger

title: .NET / C# ASP.NET Core CLAUDE.md
description: For .NET backend services and APIs using ASP.NET Core, Entity Framework Core, and Clean Architecture principles.
prompt:
# .NET / ASP.NET Core Project

## Tech Stack
- .NET 9, C# 13
- ASP.NET Core Web API
- Entity Framework Core + PostgreSQL
- MediatR for CQRS
- FluentValidation for validation
- xUnit + FluentAssertions + TestContainers

## Commands
```bash
dotnet build                                    # Build
dotnet test                                     # Run tests
dotnet run --project src/Api                    # Dev server
dotnet ef migrations add <Name> --project src/Infrastructure --startup-project src/Api
dotnet ef database update --project src/Infrastructure --startup-project src/Api
```

## Project Structure (Clean Architecture)
```
src/
  Api/           # Controllers, middleware, DI setup
  Application/   # Commands, queries, handlers (MediatR)
  Domain/        # Entities, value objects, domain events
  Infrastructure # EF Core, external services
tests/
  Unit/
  Integration/
```

## Architecture Rules
- Domain has zero external dependencies
- Application depends only on Domain
- Infrastructure implements Application interfaces
- Api depends on Application (never Infrastructure directly)

## Code Style
- PascalCase for public members, `_camelCase` for private fields
- 4-space indentation
- XML doc comments on all public APIs
- Nullable reference types enabled (`<Nullable>enable</Nullable>`)

## PATTERNS WE DON'T USE (Never Suggest)
- Repository pattern (use EF Core directly in handlers)
- AutoMapper (use manual mapping or Mapperly)
- Exceptions for business logic control flow (use Result<T>)
- Stored procedures (use LINQ)

## Forbidden Patterns
- No `var` for non-obvious types
- No synchronous database calls (always async)
- No catching `Exception` base type
- No hardcoded connection strings

title: Full-Stack Web Application CLAUDE.md
description: For monorepo full-stack applications with a React frontend and Node.js backend in the same repository.
prompt:
# Full-Stack Web Application

## Architecture
- Frontend: React + TypeScript + Vite
- Backend: Node.js + Fastify + TypeScript
- Database: PostgreSQL (Drizzle ORM)
- Auth: JWT (access + refresh token pattern)
- Package manager: pnpm workspaces

## Repository Structure
```
packages/
  web/       # React frontend (port 3000)
  api/       # Fastify backend (port 8000)
  shared/    # Shared types and utilities
```

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev              # Start both frontend and backend
pnpm dev:web          # Frontend only
pnpm dev:api          # Backend only
pnpm test             # All tests
pnpm build            # Production build
pnpm db:migrate       # Run DB migrations
pnpm db:seed          # Seed development data
```

## API Design
- Base URL: `/api/v1/`
- REST conventions: nouns, proper HTTP verbs
- All responses: `{ data, error, meta }` envelope
- Error format: `{ code, message, details }`
- OpenAPI spec generated at `/api/docs`

## Shared Types
- All API request/response types live in `packages/shared/types`
- Frontend imports from `@shared/types` — never re-define
- Zod schemas in shared package, used for both validation and type inference

## Environment Variables
- `.env.example` documents all required variables
- Frontend: `VITE_` prefix for browser-exposed vars
- Backend: never expose backend secrets to frontend

## Auth Flow
- JWT access token (15 min) + refresh token (7 days)
- Access token in memory, refresh token in httpOnly cookie
- Refresh endpoint: `POST /api/auth/refresh`

title: REST API Development CLAUDE.md
description: For dedicated REST API services with OpenAPI documentation, authentication, rate limiting, and comprehensive testing.
prompt:
# REST API Service

## API Design Conventions
- Base path: `/api/v1/`
- Plural nouns for collections: `/users`, `/orders`
- HTTP verbs: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (update), DELETE
- Response envelope: `{ data: T | null, error: string | null, meta?: PaginationMeta }`
- Error codes follow format: `DOMAIN_ERROR_NAME` (e.g., `AUTH_INVALID_TOKEN`)

## Pagination
- Cursor-based pagination for large collections
- Query params: `?cursor=<token>&limit=20`
- Response meta: `{ next_cursor, has_more, total_count }`

## Authentication
- Bearer token in `Authorization` header
- API key support via `X-API-Key` header
- All endpoints require auth unless decorated with `@Public`

## Rate Limiting
- Default: 100 req/min per IP
- Authenticated: 1000 req/min per user
- Headers returned: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
- 429 response with `Retry-After` header

## Validation
- All inputs validated with Zod at route entry point
- Return 422 with field-level errors for validation failures
- Sanitize all string inputs — trim whitespace

## Documentation
- OpenAPI 3.1 spec auto-generated from route definitions
- Every endpoint has description, request/response schemas, and error codes documented
- Postman collection exported on build

## Testing Requirements
- Unit: service and business logic
- Integration: full request → response cycle with real database
- Contract: OpenAPI spec validated against actual responses
- Load: k6 baseline script for critical endpoints

title: Microservices Architecture CLAUDE.md
description: For distributed systems with multiple independent services, event-driven communication, and service mesh.
prompt:
# Microservices Architecture

## Service Inventory
| Service | Port | Responsibility | Owner |
|---------|------|---------------|-------|
| api-gateway | 8080 | Routing, auth | Platform |
| user-service | 8081 | User management | Team A |
| order-service | 8082 | Orders | Team B |
| notification-service | 8083 | Emails/SMS | Team A |

## Communication Patterns
- **Sync (HTTP)**: For queries and commands requiring immediate response
- **Async (Kafka)**: For events and operations that can be eventually consistent
- Never synchronous calls from core services to notification service

## Event Schema Registry
- All events defined in `packages/events/` with Avro schemas
- Topic naming: `domain.entity.action` (e.g., `orders.order.created`)
- Consumers must handle schema evolution — never break consumers

## Database Per Service
- Each service owns its database — no cross-service DB access
- Data needed across services via API calls or event-driven sync
- Never share database schemas between services

## Local Development
```bash
docker compose up           # All services
docker compose up user-service  # Single service
./scripts/seed-all.sh       # Seed all databases
```

## Observability Stack
- Distributed tracing: OpenTelemetry → Jaeger (http://localhost:16686)
- Metrics: Prometheus → Grafana (http://localhost:3000)
- Logs: structured JSON → ELK (http://localhost:5601)
- All services must emit `trace_id` in every log line

## Resiliency Patterns
- Circuit breaker on all HTTP client calls (via resilience4j or similar)
- Retry with exponential backoff for transient failures
- Timeout: max 5s for any synchronous service call
- Dead letter queue for failed Kafka messages

## Deployment
- Each service has its own `Dockerfile` and `k8s/` manifests
- Services deployed independently — no coordinated releases
- Feature flags for cross-service changes

title: DevOps / Infrastructure as Code CLAUDE.md
description: For Terraform, Kubernetes, and cloud infrastructure projects. Covers state management, module conventions, and GitOps workflows.
prompt:
# DevOps / Infrastructure as Code

## IaC Tooling
- Terraform 1.9+ with OpenTofu as alternative
- State: S3 backend + DynamoDB locking
- Module registry: private Terraform registry at registry.internal.company.com
- Kubernetes: EKS 1.30, manifests managed via Helm 3

## Terraform Workflow
```bash
terraform init          # Initialize
terraform validate      # Validate configuration
terraform plan          # Preview changes (always before apply)
terraform apply         # Apply (requires explicit approval)
terraform destroy       # DESTRUCTIVE — requires explicit approval and reason
```

## Module Structure
```
modules/
  vpc/
  eks/
  rds/
environments/
  dev/
  staging/
  prod/
```

## Naming Conventions
- Resources: `{env}-{region}-{service}-{purpose}` (e.g., `prod-us-east-1-api-lb`)
- Tags required: `Environment`, `Team`, `CostCenter`, `ManagedBy=terraform`
- Kubernetes namespaces: `{team}-{env}` (e.g., `platform-prod`)

## Secret Management
- Secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager
- Never in Terraform state, environment variables, or code
- Kubernetes secrets injected via External Secrets Operator

## GitOps (ArgoCD)
- All K8s changes via Git — never `kubectl apply` directly to prod
- ArgoCD UI: https://argocd.internal.company.com
- Sync policy: auto-sync in dev, manual in prod

## PROHIBITED ACTIONS
- Never `terraform apply` to prod without a plan review
- Never `terraform destroy` without explicit written approval
- Never push directly to `main` branch
- Never hardcode account IDs, region names, or AMI IDs

title: Data Science / Analytics CLAUDE.md
description: For Python-based data analysis projects using pandas, Jupyter notebooks, and statistical modeling. Covers reproducibility and data privacy.
prompt:
# Data Science / Analytics Project

## Environment
- Python 3.12 managed by `uv`
- Core: pandas 2.x, NumPy 2.x, matplotlib, seaborn
- Modeling: scikit-learn, statsmodels
- Notebooks: JupyterLab

## Commands
```bash
uv sync                  # Install dependencies
uv run jupyter lab       # Start JupyterLab
uv run pytest tests/     # Run data pipeline tests
```

## Project Structure
```
data/
  raw/          # Immutable raw data — never modify
  interim/      # Intermediate transformed data
  processed/    # Final, analysis-ready datasets
  external/     # External reference data
notebooks/
  01_explore/   # Exploratory analysis
  02_analyze/   # Core analysis
  03_visualize/ # Report visualizations
src/
  features/     # Feature engineering
  models/       # Model training and evaluation
  utils/        # Shared utilities
```

## Reproducibility Requirements
- Set random seeds at the top of every notebook: `np.random.seed(42)`
- Pin all dependency versions in `pyproject.toml`
- Never load data from absolute paths — use relative paths from project root
- Document data sources and access instructions in `data/README.md`

## Notebook Conventions
- One notebook per analysis question
- Clear cell output before committing
- Markdown cells to explain reasoning, not just code comments
- First cell: imports and configuration; last cell: summary/conclusions

## Data Privacy
- Never commit raw PII data to the repository
- Anonymize before adding to `data/` directory
- Log all data access for audit purposes
- No production data in development notebooks

## Visualization
- Preferred: matplotlib/seaborn for static, plotly for interactive
- Always include axis labels, titles, and units
- Save figures to `reports/figures/` as PNG and SVG

title: Machine Learning / AI Projects CLAUDE.md
description: For ML model development, training pipelines, and AI systems using PyTorch or scikit-learn with experiment tracking.
prompt:
# Machine Learning / AI Project

## Tech Stack
- Python 3.12, PyTorch 2.x
- Experiment tracking: MLflow (server at http://localhost:5000)
- Config management: Hydra
- Data versioning: DVC
- Serving: FastAPI wrapper over model

## Commands
```bash
uv sync                          # Install dependencies
python src/train.py              # Train with default config
python src/train.py +experiment=my_exp  # Custom experiment config
mlflow ui                        # View experiment dashboard
dvc pull                         # Download latest dataset version
dvc push                         # Push new dataset version
```

## Project Structure
```
config/
  model/        # Model hyperparameters
  training/     # Training configuration
  data/         # Data loading configuration
data/           # DVC-tracked datasets
src/
  data/         # Dataset classes and loaders
  models/       # Model architectures
  training/     # Training loops
  evaluation/   # Metrics and evaluation
  inference/    # Serving code
checkpoints/    # Model checkpoints (gitignored)
```

## Experiment Tracking Rules
- Every training run logged to MLflow
- Log: hyperparameters, metrics per epoch, final model artifact
- Tag experiments with `git_commit` and `dataset_version`
- Never overwrite existing experiment runs

## Reproducibility
- Seeds: `torch.manual_seed(42)`, `random.seed(42)`, `np.random.seed(42)`
- Document GPU/hardware requirements in `README.md`
- Save model architecture alongside weights
- Use deterministic operations: `torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)` in tests

## Model Versioning
- Production models registered in MLflow Model Registry
- Staging → Production promotion requires evaluation report
- Never delete model versions — archive instead

title: React Native / Mobile CLAUDE.md
description: For cross-platform mobile applications built with React Native and TypeScript targeting iOS and Android.
prompt:
# React Native Mobile App

## Tech Stack
- React Native 0.76 + Expo SDK 52
- TypeScript (strict)
- React Navigation 7 (stack + tabs)
- Zustand for state management
- TanStack Query for server state
- Detox for E2E testing

## Commands
```bash
pnpm start            # Metro bundler
pnpm ios              # iOS simulator
pnpm android          # Android emulator
pnpm test             # Jest unit tests
pnpm e2e:ios          # Detox E2E (iOS)
pnpm e2e:android      # Detox E2E (Android)
```

## Project Structure
```
src/
  screens/      # Screen components
  components/   # Reusable UI components
  navigation/   # React Navigation setup
  hooks/        # Custom hooks
  store/        # Zustand stores
  services/     # API and native services
  utils/        # Helpers
assets/         # Images, fonts, icons
```

## Navigation
- Root navigator: `RootNavigator` in `navigation/RootNavigator.tsx`
- Auth flow separated from main app stack
- Deep links configured in `app.json` and handled in RootNavigator
- Screen names typed via `@react-navigation/native`

## Platform-Specific Code
- Use `Platform.OS === 'ios'` for minor differences
- Create `.ios.tsx` / `.android.tsx` files for major differences
- Document any native module requirements in `NATIVE_MODULES.md`

## Performance
- List components must use `FlashList` (not `FlatList`)
- Avoid anonymous functions in render methods
- Use `React.memo` on expensive components
- Profile with Flipper before optimizing

## Security
- Secrets stored in Expo SecureStore (never AsyncStorage)
- Certificate pinning for API calls
- No sensitive data in logs in production builds

title: Monorepo (Turborepo) CLAUDE.md
description: For large monorepos managed with Turborepo and pnpm workspaces containing multiple apps and shared packages.
prompt:
# Monorepo (Turborepo + pnpm Workspaces)

## Repository Map
```
apps/
  web/          # Next.js marketing site
  app/          # Next.js main application
  api/          # Node.js API server
packages/
  ui/           # Shared React component library
  config/       # Shared ESLint, TypeScript, Tailwind configs
  types/        # Shared TypeScript types
  utils/        # Shared utility functions
  db/           # Drizzle ORM schema and client
```

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev                        # All apps in parallel
pnpm dev --filter=app           # Single app
pnpm build                      # All apps (respects Turbo cache)
pnpm test                       # All tests
pnpm lint                       # All packages
pnpm add lodash --filter=web    # Add dep to specific package
pnpm add @company/ui --filter=app --workspace  # Add internal dep
```

## Dependency Rules
- Apps can depend on packages — never the reverse
- Packages must not depend on apps
- No circular dependencies between packages
- `packages/config` has no internal deps — it's the foundation

## Package Conventions
- All packages export from `src/index.ts`
- Package name: `@company/{name}` in package.json
- Every package has its own `tsconfig.json` extending `@company/config/tsconfig-base.json`

## Adding a New Package
1. Create directory under `packages/`
2. Copy `package.json` from existing package
3. Update name, description, and dependencies
4. Run `pnpm install` from root
5. Add CLAUDE.md to the package with package-specific instructions

## Key Decisions
| Decision | Choice | Date | Reason |
|----------|--------|------|--------|
| Package manager | pnpm | 2024-01 | Disk efficiency, strict deps |
| Build system | Turborepo | 2024-01 | Caching, parallelism |
| Component lib | Internal (@company/ui) | 2024-03 | Custom design system |

title: Test-Driven Development (TDD) CLAUDE.md
description: For projects following strict Red-Green-Refactor methodology where tests are written before implementation.
prompt:
# Test-Driven Development Project

## TDD Mandate
**Always write a failing test before writing implementation code.**
The Red-Green-Refactor cycle is non-negotiable:
1. RED: Write a failing test that describes the desired behavior
2. GREEN: Write the minimum code to make the test pass
3. REFACTOR: Clean up without breaking tests

## Testing Stack
- Framework: Vitest
- Assertions: Vitest native + `@testing-library/react` for UI
- Mocking: Vitest mocks (never `jest.mock` patterns)
- Property testing: `fast-check`
- Mutation testing: Stryker (CI gate: mutation score ≥ 80%)

## Commands
```bash
pnpm test               # Run all tests
pnpm test:watch         # Watch mode (use during development)
pnpm test:coverage      # Coverage report
pnpm test:mutation      # Stryker mutation testing
pnpm test src/foo.test.ts  # Single file
```

## Coverage Requirements
- Line coverage: ≥ 95%
- Branch coverage: ≥ 90%
- Coverage enforced in CI — build fails if below threshold

## Test Naming
```typescript
describe('UserService', () => {
  describe('createUser', () => {
    it('creates user with valid email and password', () => {});
    it('throws ValidationError when email is invalid', () => {});
    it('throws DuplicateError when email already exists', () => {});
  });
});
```

## Isolation Rules
- No shared mutable state between tests
- Tests must be order-independent (use `beforeEach` to reset state)
- Never use real network calls — mock external services
- Database tests use transactions rolled back after each test

## Anti-Patterns (Forbidden)
- Writing implementation before tests
- Testing implementation details (internal functions)
- Over-mocking (mocking what you own)
- Skipping the Red phase (making a test green before it fails)
- `.skip` or `.only` in committed code

title: Security-Focused Application CLAUDE.md
description: For security-critical applications. Enforces OWASP Top 10 compliance, dependency scanning, and secure coding patterns.
prompt:
# Security-Focused Application

## Security Standards
- OWASP Top 10 (2021) compliance required
- NIST Cybersecurity Framework aligned
- Regular third-party penetration testing (quarterly)

## Cryptography
- Hashing passwords: `bcrypt` (cost factor ≥ 12) or `argon2id`
- Encryption at rest: AES-256-GCM
- TLS: 1.3 minimum, 1.2 with strong ciphers only
- NEVER: MD5, SHA-1 for security purposes, RC4, DES
- NEVER roll your own crypto — use audited libraries only

## Authentication & Authorization
- JWT: signed with RS256 (asymmetric), not HS256
- Tokens: access (15 min), refresh (7 days, rotated on use)
- MFA required for admin accounts
- Rate limit login endpoint: 5 attempts per minute per IP
- Lockout after 10 failed attempts

## Input Validation (All User Input is Untrusted)
- Allowlist validation — never blocklist
- Parameterized queries — never string concatenation for SQL
- Output encoding — escape for the output context (HTML, JS, URL, SQL)
- File uploads: validate MIME type from content (not extension), virus scan

## Secrets Management
- Stored in: AWS Secrets Manager / HashiCorp Vault
- Never in: code, environment variables committed to Git, logs
- Rotation: automated for all service credentials (90-day max)
- Audit logging for all secret access

## Security Review Checklist (Pre-Merge)
- [ ] No new dependencies without security review
- [ ] `npm audit` / `pip audit` passes with no high/critical
- [ ] No secrets in diff
- [ ] Sensitive data not in logs
- [ ] New API endpoints have auth and authorization checks

## SAST / DAST Tools
- SAST: Semgrep (rules in `.semgrep/`), CodeQL (GitHub Actions)
- Dependency: Snyk in CI
- DAST: OWASP ZAP in staging before production releases

title: Solo Developer / Indie Project CLAUDE.md
description: Lightweight CLAUDE.md for solo developers or indie hackers. Focuses on speed, pragmatism, and self-accountability.
prompt:
# [Project Name] — Solo Dev Setup

## What This Is
[One sentence description of what the project does and why it exists]

Target user: [describe your target user]
MVP goal: [what does v1.0 look like?]

## Tech Stack
- Frontend: [your choice]
- Backend: [your choice]
- Database: [your choice]
- Hosting: [your choice]

## Quick Commands
```bash
npm run dev     # Start local development
npm run build   # Build for production
npm test        # Run tests
npm run deploy  # Deploy to production
```

## Key Files
- `src/lib/db.ts` — database client
- `src/lib/auth.ts` — auth utilities
- `src/env.ts` — environment validation

## Integrations & External Services
- Auth: [e.g., Clerk — docs at clerk.com]
- Payments: [e.g., Stripe — test keys in .env.local]
- Email: [e.g., Resend]
- Analytics: [e.g., PostHog]

## In-Scope (MVP)
- [Feature 1]
- [Feature 2]
- [Feature 3]

## OUT OF SCOPE (Don't build this yet)
- Multi-tenancy
- Mobile apps
- API for third parties
- Complex permissions

## Self-Accountability Rules
- Ship before polishing
- No feature creep — new ideas go in BACKLOG.md
- Spend < 1 day on any task before reassessing
- No perfectionism on internal tooling

## Key Decisions Made
| Decision | Choice | Why |
|----------|--------|-----|
| [what] | [choice] | [reason] |

title: Open Source Library CLAUDE.md
description: For public npm/PyPI/crates.io packages. Covers API stability, changelog conventions, and contributor guidelines.
prompt:
# Open Source Library

## About This Library
[One-line description of what the library does]

Published to: npm as `@org/package-name`
Minimum supported: Node 20+, TypeScript 5.0+
Bundle size target: < 10KB gzipped

## Public API Contract
- Everything in `src/index.ts` is public API — changes are breaking if they remove or change signatures
- Everything in `src/internal/` is NOT public API — can change any time
- Mark unstable features with `@experimental` JSDoc tag

## Versioning (Semantic Versioning strictly)
- PATCH: bug fixes, non-breaking
- MINOR: new features, backward compatible
- MAJOR: breaking changes (require migration guide)
- Never break consumers in a minor/patch release

## Commands
```bash
pnpm test               # Run tests
pnpm build              # Build dist/
pnpm changeset          # Create changeset for PR
pnpm release            # Publish (CI only — never manually)
pnpm size               # Check bundle size
```

## Documentation Requirements
- All public APIs: JSDoc with `@param`, `@returns`, `@throws`, `@example`
- Examples must be runnable (tested with `doctest` or similar)
- CHANGELOG.md follows Keep a Changelog format
- README covers: install, quick start, full API reference, migration guide

## Contribution Requirements
- New features require tests (coverage must not decrease)
- Bug fixes require a regression test
- API additions require documentation
- Breaking changes require a migration guide in the PR

## Release Process
1. PR merged to `main`
2. Changeset created with `pnpm changeset`
3. Release PR auto-created by Changesets bot
4. Merge release PR → auto-publish to npm

## Tree Shaking
- Side-effect-free: `"sideEffects": false` in package.json
- Named exports only — no default exports
- No top-level code with side effects

title: Static Site / Documentation Site CLAUDE.md
description: For sites built with Astro, Hugo, VitePress, or Docusaurus. Covers content structure, frontmatter, and deployment.
prompt:
# Static Documentation Site (Astro)

## Tech Stack
- Astro 5.x + TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS + MDX for content
- Deployed to: Vercel / Cloudflare Pages

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev          # Local dev (http://localhost:4321)
pnpm build        # Production build
pnpm preview      # Preview production build locally
```

## Content Structure
```
src/
  content/
    docs/         # Documentation pages
    blog/         # Blog posts
    changelog/    # Changelog entries
  pages/          # Astro pages
  components/     # UI components
  layouts/        # Page layouts
public/           # Static assets (images, fonts)
```

## Frontmatter Schema
All docs pages require:
```yaml
---
title: "Page Title"
description: "SEO description (150-160 chars)"
pubDate: 2026-01-15
category: "guides" # or "reference" | "tutorials"
---
```

## Content Conventions
- H1 comes from frontmatter `title` — don't repeat in body
- Use `##` (H2) for main sections, `###` (H3) for subsections
- Never skip heading levels
- Code blocks must specify language for syntax highlighting
- Images: WebP format, stored in `public/images/`, alt text required

## SEO Requirements
- Every page needs unique `description` frontmatter
- OG image: auto-generated from title using `og-image.ts`
- Canonical URLs set automatically by Astro
- Sitemap auto-generated at build time

## Deployment
- Merge to `main` → auto-deploy to production
- Preview deployments for all PRs
- Redirects managed in `vercel.json` / `_redirects`

title: CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions) CLAUDE.md
description: For projects with GitHub Actions workflows covering build, test, security scanning, and deployment automation.
prompt:
# CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions)

## Pipeline Overview
All workflows in `.github/workflows/`:

| Workflow | Trigger | Duration | Purpose |
|----------|---------|----------|---------|
| `ci.yml` | Every PR, push | ~5 min | Lint, test, build |
| `security.yml` | Nightly | ~10 min | SAST, dependency audit |
| `release.yml` | Tag `v*` | ~15 min | Build, push image, deploy |
| `preview.yml` | PR to main | ~8 min | Deploy preview environment |

## CI Pipeline Stages
```
lint → type-check → test → build → docker-build → security-scan
```
All stages run in parallel where possible. Any failure blocks merge.

## Branch Strategy
- `main`: production, protected (no direct push)
- `develop`: staging environment
- `feature/*`: feature branches (short-lived)
- PRs require: 1 approval + all checks green

## Docker
- Multi-stage Dockerfile: `builder` → `runner`
- Images tagged: `{sha}`, `{branch}`, `latest` (on main only)
- Registry: GitHub Container Registry (`ghcr.io/{org}/{repo}`)
- Image scanning: Trivy in CI, block on CRITICAL vulnerabilities

## Secrets
- All secrets in GitHub Actions secrets (never in workflow files)
- Environment-scoped secrets for staging vs prod
- Naming convention: `{ENV}_{SERVICE}_{KEY}` (e.g., `PROD_DB_PASSWORD`)

## Deployment Strategy
- Dev: automatic on push to `develop`
- Staging: automatic on merge to `main`
- Production: manual approval gate after staging

## Rollback
```bash
# Rollback production to previous version:
gh workflow run rollback.yml -f version=<SHA>
```

## Notifications
- Slack `#deployments` channel: all production deploys
- Slack `#alerts` channel: failed CI on main branch
- PagerDuty: production deploy failures

title: High Performance / Low Latency CLAUDE.md
description: For latency-sensitive, high-throughput systems where performance is a primary requirement. Enforces profiling-first and measurement-driven optimization.
prompt:
# High Performance System

## Performance Targets
| Endpoint | P50 | P95 | P99 | Throughput |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|------------|
| GET /api/feed | < 20ms | < 50ms | < 100ms | 10k RPS |
| POST /api/events | < 5ms | < 15ms | < 30ms | 50k RPS |

## PERFORMANCE RULE #1
**Measure before optimizing.** Never optimize based on intuition.
Required: profile first, identify bottleneck, measure improvement.

## Profiling Tools
```bash
# CPU profiling
node --prof app.js
node --prof-process isolate-*.log > profile.txt

# Memory profiling
clinic.js heap -- node app.js

# Load testing
k6 run scripts/load-test.js
```

## Caching Strategy
- L1: In-process LRU (lru-cache) — sub-millisecond, limited size
- L2: Redis — ~1ms, distributed, configurable TTL
- L3: CDN (Cloudflare) — for public, cacheable responses
- Cache-aside pattern — application manages cache population
- Document TTL rationale for every cached entity

## Hot Path Rules
- No allocations in hot paths (reuse objects/buffers)
- No synchronous I/O
- No logging in hot paths (use sampling)
- No dynamic property access on frequently accessed objects

## Database Optimization
- Connection pool size: configured per service based on load tests
- All queries have EXPLAIN ANALYZE run and documented
- No N+1 queries — use DataLoader or batch queries
- Read replicas for analytics queries

## Performance Regression Prevention
- Benchmark suite runs in CI for critical paths
- Any PR that degrades P99 by > 10% is blocked
- Weekly performance review comparing metrics to baseline

title: Agile / Scrum Team CLAUDE.md
description: For teams working in sprints with backlogs, PRs, and iterative delivery. Defines DoD, DoR, branching, and review standards.
prompt:
# [Team Name] — Engineering Standards

## Sprint Cadence
- Sprint length: 2 weeks
- Sprint ceremonies: Monday planning, Friday demo + retro
- Current sprint board: [link to Jira/Linear board]

## Definition of Done
A feature is DONE when:
- [ ] Code is reviewed and approved by ≥ 2 team members
- [ ] All tests pass (unit + integration)
- [ ] Test coverage maintained (≥ 80%)
- [ ] Documentation updated (if user-facing change)
- [ ] Deployed to staging and smoke-tested
- [ ] Product Owner has accepted

## Definition of Ready
A ticket is READY for sprint when:
- [ ] Clear acceptance criteria written
- [ ] Designs attached (if UI work)
- [ ] Dependencies identified
- [ ] Story points estimated

## Branching Strategy
```
main          # Production (protected)
  └── develop # Integration branch
        └── feature/TICKET-123-short-description
        └── bugfix/TICKET-456-fix-name
        └── hotfix/TICKET-789-critical-fix  # Branches from main
```

## Commit Message Format (Conventional Commits)
```
feat(auth): add OAuth2 login with Google
fix(cart): resolve race condition on concurrent checkout
chore(deps): update react to 19.x
docs(api): add endpoint documentation for /users
test(cart): add integration tests for checkout flow
```

## Pull Request Requirements
- PR title follows Conventional Commits format
- PR body: What, Why, How to test, Screenshots (for UI changes)
- Minimum 2 approvals required
- All CI checks must pass
- No self-merge

## Code Review Etiquette
- Review within 24 hours of assignment
- Use `nit:` prefix for non-blocking style comments
- Approve only when you'd be comfortable owning the code
- Resolve your own comments after addressing them

title: Compliance / Regulated Industry CLAUDE.md
description: For HIPAA, GDPR, SOX, or PCI-DSS regulated software. Enforces audit logging, data classification, and encryption requirements.
prompt:
# Regulated Industry Application

## Applicable Regulations
- [x] HIPAA (Protected Health Information)
- [ ] GDPR (EU Personal Data)
- [ ] PCI-DSS (Payment Card Data)
- [ ] SOX (Financial Reporting)

## Data Classification
| Class | Examples | Handling |
|-------|---------|----------|
| PHI/PII | Names, DOB, SSN, medical records | Encrypted at rest + transit, access logged |
| Sensitive | Internal business data | Encrypted at rest, restricted access |
| Internal | Code, configs | Standard security controls |
| Public | Marketing content | No special handling |

## Encryption Requirements
- At rest: AES-256 (keys managed in AWS KMS)
- In transit: TLS 1.3 (never 1.0/1.1)
- Database: column-level encryption for PHI fields
- Backups: encrypted with separate key rotation

## Audit Logging (REQUIRED for all PHI access)
Every access to PHI must log:
```json
{
  "timestamp": "ISO8601",
  "user_id": "...",
  "action": "read|write|delete",
  "resource_type": "patient_record",
  "resource_id": "...",
  "ip_address": "...",
  "reason": "treatment|payment|operations"
}
```
Audit logs: immutable, retained 7 years, stored separately from application logs.

## Access Control
- Role-based access control (RBAC) — principle of least privilege
- MFA required for all staff accessing PHI
- Session timeout: 15 minutes of inactivity
- All privileged access: just-in-time with approval workflow

## ABSOLUTE PROHIBITIONS
- Never log PHI (names, DOBs, diagnoses) in application logs
- Never store PHI in unencrypted databases or files
- Never transmit PHI over HTTP (only HTTPS)
- Never include PHI in URLs or query parameters
- Never store PHI in browser localStorage or cookies

## Breach Response
If potential breach detected:
1. Immediately notify Security team (security@company.com)
2. Do NOT attempt to investigate alone
3. Preserve all logs — do not delete anything
4. 72-hour notification requirement to HHS (HIPAA)

title: Domain-Driven Design (DDD) CLAUDE.md
description: For complex business domain software using DDD principles with bounded contexts, aggregates, and ubiquitous language.
prompt:
# Domain-Driven Design Project

## Ubiquitous Language Glossary
| Term | Definition in This Context |
|------|---------------------------|
| Order | A confirmed purchase request from a Customer |
| Customer | A registered user who has completed at least one Order |
| Fulfillment | The process of preparing and shipping an Order |
| SKU | A unique identifier for a Product variant |

**Use these exact terms in code** — no synonyms, no abbreviations.

## Bounded Contexts
| Context | Responsibility | Team | Database |
|---------|---------------|------|----------|
| Ordering | Order lifecycle | Team A | orders_db |
| Inventory | Stock management | Team B | inventory_db |
| Shipping | Fulfillment | Team C | shipping_db |
| Identity | Authentication | Platform | identity_db |

## Architecture (Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters)
```
src/
  domain/       # Entities, value objects, aggregates, domain events
  application/  # Use cases, command/query handlers
  ports/        # Interfaces (inbound + outbound)
  adapters/
    inbound/    # REST controllers, event consumers
    outbound/   # Database repositories, external APIs
```

## Aggregate Rules
- Aggregates enforce invariants within their boundary
- Only communicate between aggregates via Domain Events
- No direct references between aggregates — reference by ID only
- One repository per aggregate root

## Domain Events
- Immutable value objects named in past tense: `OrderPlaced`, `InventoryReserved`
- Raised within aggregates, published by application layer
- Event schema in `src/domain/events/`
- Never modify a published event schema — add a new version

## FORBIDDEN PATTERNS (Anti-Corruption)
- Anemic domain models (entities with only getters/setters)
- Database schema leaking into domain
- Cross-aggregate transactions
- Domain logic in controllers or repositories
- Using bounded context terms outside their context

title: Ruby on Rails CLAUDE.md
description: For Rails applications following convention-over-configuration with RSpec, Hotwire/Turbo, and the Rails ecosystem.
prompt:
# Ruby on Rails Application

## Tech Stack
- Ruby 3.4, Rails 8.x
- PostgreSQL (ActiveRecord)
- Hotwire (Turbo + Stimulus) for frontend
- Solid Queue for background jobs
- Devise for authentication
- RSpec + FactoryBot + Capybara for testing

## Commands
```bash
bin/dev               # Start dev server (foreman)
bundle exec rspec     # Run tests
bundle exec rubocop   # Lint
bin/rails db:migrate  # Run migrations
bin/rails db:seed     # Seed database
bin/rails console     # Rails console
```

## Rails Conventions (Follow strictly)
- Fat models, skinny controllers
- Service objects for complex business logic (`app/services/`)
- Concerns for shared model/controller behavior
- Presenters for view logic (`app/presenters/`)
- Policy objects for authorization (Pundit)

## ActiveRecord
- Always add database-level constraints (not just model validations)
- Index all foreign keys and frequently queried columns
- Use `find_each` for bulk operations (never `all.each`)
- Avoid N+1 queries — use `includes`, `preload`, or `eager_load`
- Never put queries in views or partials

## Testing (RSpec)
- Model specs: test validations, associations, scopes, instance methods
- Request specs: test API endpoints end-to-end
- System specs: Capybara for user-facing flows
- No mocks for database — use FactoryBot with real records
- Use VCR cassettes for external HTTP calls

## Background Jobs
- All jobs inherit from `ApplicationJob`
- Jobs must be idempotent
- Retry configuration: max 5 attempts with exponential backoff
- No business logic in jobs — delegate to service objects

## Code Style (RuboCop)
- RuboCop config in `.rubocop.yml`
- No offense disabling without a comment explaining why
- Prefer `&method(:foo)` over `{ |x| foo(x) }`

title: Embedded Systems / Firmware CLAUDE.md
description: For microcontroller firmware, RTOS-based systems, and hardware-adjacent software in C/C++ with strict resource constraints.
prompt:
# Embedded Systems / Firmware

## Hardware Target
- MCU: STM32H743 (Arm Cortex-M7, 480 MHz)
- Flash: 2MB | RAM: 1MB SRAM
- RTOS: FreeRTOS 10.6
- Toolchain: arm-none-eabi-gcc 13.x

## Commands
```bash
cmake -B build -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=toolchain.cmake
cmake --build build         # Compile
cmake --build build --target flash  # Flash to target
ctest --test-dir build      # Run tests (host-based)
```

## Memory Constraints
- Stack per task: max 2KB (document if more needed)
- No heap allocation after init (use static or memory pools)
- All buffers statically sized and declared at compile time
- Stack usage analyzed with `-fstack-usage` in CI

## ISR Rules (Interrupt Service Routines)
- ISRs must complete in < 1µs
- No blocking calls in ISRs
- No dynamic memory allocation in ISRs
- Communicate via FreeRTOS queues or event groups only
- Prefix ISR functions with `ISR_` to make them identifiable

## RTOS Conventions
- Tasks defined in `tasks/` directory
- Priority documented at task creation
- All tasks have watchdog registration
- Task stack size validated at startup

## Code Style (MISRA-C adjacent)
- No implicit type conversions — explicit casts required
- No dynamic memory allocation in production code (`malloc`/`new` forbidden)
- All switch statements have `default:` case
- All function return values checked
- `volatile` on all hardware register variables

## Forbidden Patterns
- `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `free` (use static allocation)
- `printf` in production (use lightweight logging via UART DMA)
- Recursive functions (stack unpredictable)
- Floating point in ISRs (FPU context not saved by default)

title: GraphQL API CLAUDE.md
description: For GraphQL APIs with schema-first design, DataLoader patterns, and comprehensive resolver testing.
prompt:
# GraphQL API

## Tech Stack
- Node.js + TypeScript
- GraphQL Yoga (or Apollo Server 4)
- Pothos for code-first schema building
- DataLoader for batching
- Prisma + PostgreSQL

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev          # Dev server with schema codegen watch
pnpm codegen      # Generate TypeScript types from schema
pnpm test         # Run resolver tests
pnpm schema:check # Check schema for breaking changes
```

## Schema Conventions
- Naming: queries `camelCase`, types `PascalCase`, enums `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`
- Connections for paginated lists (Relay-style cursor pagination)
- Mutations return the mutated object: `createUser` returns `User`
- Error handling: union types (`UserResult = User | UserError`)
- Every field that can be null is explicitly `nullable`

## Resolver Rules
- Resolvers are thin — business logic in service layer
- Never access database directly in resolvers — use repositories
- Use DataLoader for any field that accesses related data
- Resolvers must not have N+1 queries

## DataLoader Pattern
```typescript
// Always batch-load related entities
const userLoader = new DataLoader(async (ids: readonly string[]) => {
  const users = await userRepo.findByIds([...ids]);
  return ids.map(id => users.find(u => u.id === id) ?? null);
});
```

## Security
- Query depth limit: max 7 levels
- Query complexity limit: max 1000 points
- Field-level authorization via Pothos auth plugin
- Persisted queries in production (no arbitrary query execution)
- Never expose internal IDs — use opaque global IDs (Relay spec)

## Schema Versioning
- Breaking changes tracked with `graphql-inspector`
- Schema changelog in `SCHEMA_CHANGELOG.md`
- Deprecate fields for 2 sprints before removing

title: AI Agents / LLM Applications CLAUDE.md
description: For applications built with Claude API or other LLMs — chatbots, agents, RAG systems, and AI-powered features.
prompt:
# AI Agents / LLM Application

## Tech Stack
- Anthropic Claude SDK (TypeScript)
- Preferred model: `claude-opus-4-6` for complex tasks, `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001` for simple/fast
- Vector store: pgvector (PostgreSQL extension)
- Embeddings: `text-embedding-3-small` (OpenAI) or Voyage AI

## Commands
```bash
pnpm dev          # Start dev server
pnpm test         # Run tests (mocks LLM calls)
pnpm test:live    # Integration tests with real API (costs money)
```

## Claude API Usage
```typescript
import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk';

const client = new Anthropic(); // Uses ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var

// Always set a system prompt
const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-opus-4-6',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  system: 'You are...',
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: userMessage }],
});
```

## Prompt Engineering Conventions
- System prompts stored in `src/prompts/` as `.md` files
- Version control prompts like code — every change committed
- Test prompts with eval suite before deploying
- Document expected behavior and edge cases for each prompt

## RAG Pipeline
1. Ingestion: chunk → embed → upsert to pgvector
2. Retrieval: embed query → similarity search → rerank
3. Generation: retrieved context + user query → Claude
- Chunk size: 512 tokens with 50-token overlap
- Top-k: 5 results for retrieval
- Reranking: Cohere rerank API

## Cost Management
- Log every API call: model, input tokens, output tokens, cost
- Budget alerts configured at 80% of monthly limit
- Cache repeated prompts (exact match) with 1-hour TTL
- Use Haiku for classification/routing, Opus for complex generation

## Safety & Guardrails
- Input guardrails: detect and block prompt injection attempts
- Output guardrails: scan for PII before returning to user
- Never pass raw user input directly to tool calls
- Human-in-the-loop for any irreversible actions
